author: @kenburrell6227
text: The decay of C-14 or Ni-63 does not involve alpha particles.  Both atoms transmute when a neutron decays into a proton and electron (beta-minus decay) and result in stable isotopes N-14 and Cu-63, respectively.   Both have long half-lives, 5700 years and 101 years, respectively.  There are also anti-neutrinos released, but they rarely interact with anything.  Americium-241 decays by alpha particles (He nuclei), which results in Neptunium-237, with a very long half-life and radioactive.   The Ni-63 battery is far less hazardous.

author: @brunonikodemski2420
text: Our company did some research work with McDonnel-Douglas company, in the 1980s era, with radio-isotope ionic flow batteries.  I still have an advertising blurb from them.  Later the Wilson-Greatbatch company actually tried these for implantable Pacemakers.  They worked fine, but had a very low current output, and were supplanted by RTGs, which had power outputs of hundreds of watts.  NASA and the Military adapted RTGs for many space programs, several deep-space probes, and similar, where 10-to-20-year power outputs had to be used.  The main problem with these devices is that they necessarily have to be very "bulky", for safety and mechanical packaging reasons.  The other problem is simple radiation damage to all of the internal materials, which cause heaving at a steady rate, much like the dendrite problem and swelling with lithium batteries.  There are NO free lunches in basic Physics.  Trust me on this.

author: @samadams2224
text: More to the point of the video, I wonder if having something like these augmented by small bank of supercapacitors setup to sequentially discharge based on demand would be the way to go in some cases. Obviously these would make a fantastic bios battery for computer (provided proper shielding) but clearly falls short in high demand applications. People often look at supercapacitors as a magic bullet and that they are not but they are really good at some things. Their ability to act as a reservoir for a trickle of power and release it in high demand strikes me as a good application here where the positive and negative aspects of both components alighn inversely to make a workable "battery". Other considerations would need to be made like disposal and the fact that the actual power cell has a life span orders of magnitude longer than any current super capacitors but I see no reason that part of the component couldn't be easily replaceable or at very least recycled to mitigate quite reasonable waste concerns.  Very cool and some history I was unaware of regardless.

author: @samadams2224
text:
Initially came here to post a bunch of negative things but you covered all the bases so well I can't really do so... Well that and the part about things needed where "a healthy disregard for your own safety" had me laughing out loud. When it comes to radioactivity and the elements associated with it, there are very much real hazards and there is a lot of irrational fears coming from a lack of understanding something so complex.  Sadly even the smartest people do dumb things. Case in point Louis Slotin and the criticality incident at los alamos. Why on earth the guy was using two screwdrivers rather than building some sort of mechanism to carry out the test is beyond me. I do understand the race to get a working device before the Nazis (something that turned out to be an irrational fear in hindsight) but the guy was well aware of the dangers involved. Sadly overestimating ones own dexterity is a thing all humans do but seldom ends with such dire consequences. 

The other point I would like to make is the fukishima disaster (and BTW it is still a disaster IMHO worse than that of Chernobyl) was ultimately caused by a lack of cooling water during catastrophic flooding brought on by a tidal wave. In short the whole facility was likely submerged in water while the core(s) were suffering at least a partial meltdown (it's hard to get real honest information on but I believe at least one of the reactors suffered a full meltdown based on the information I have been able to find as it's not forthcoming). Not to say it would be harmless and it's easy to say in hindsight but at minimum the act of simply leaving doors open and letting the ocean water into the reactors would have bought more time to deal with the situation at minimum. Ultimately the backup diesel generators were disabled by the initial tidal wave and those were what would have been pumping water in a well thought out redundant safety system that missed one very critical consideration. 

In short, radioactive things deserve a healthy concern but not irrational fears but it's hard to convince people their fears are irrational when they point to things like what I have mentioned. Nuclear power has many positives but when it goes wrong it goes tremendously wrong. I can't recall where I heard it but the analogy "Other than that, how was the play Mrs. Lincoln?" is terse, to the point and quite funny depending on your humor level. I understand that not everyone watching youtube resides in the us and that comment is in relation to the assassination of president Lincoln... I think most would make the connection but understand some might not and want the point I am making to be at least conveyed properly. 

In short fantastic video on a new technology I was unaware of as well as the history behind it. Subscribed and will be checking out your other offerings in my limited free time. Thanks for taking the time to put all this together. Most of the television programs on the subject matter fall well short of this videos quality in substance. Oddly enough it's off the cuff humor like yours that peaks my interest enough to keep watching. In this case there was enough substantial  info prior I would have finished anyways. I think there is something in human nature that forces us to laugh at the most serious of things.I often see someone with expert knowledge make a joke in what some would consider poor taste or at very least a tremendous understatement. It often conveys that A. Don't forget to keep a since of humor even when dealing with the most serious of things. B. I am making a joke but at the same time I am deadly serious.  You can beat people over the head with information or you can say something off color enough that it possibly bothers them or at very least makes them wonder why such a knowledgeable person would be so flippant with something so deadly serious. Well the person is not flippant and often the brightest people are often not fun to listen to. Folks like Richard Feynman who were no doubt brilliant but also appreciated humor enough to dot his lectures with it make complex and hard to understand subject matters at least more digestible. He was also quick to tell people (in some cases) "If that bothers you, that's okay. It bothers everybody including the scientists working on it and ultimately no one really knows for certain... But if you want meaningful answers to real world problems you'll just have to accept that as the best we can offer right now and work with it." I'm paraphrasing here and not making a verbatim quote but can point to lectures and interviews were he said something quite similar. 

Anyways, its a good thing not to take ourselves to seriously and your ability to do so was the icing on the cake of a great video full of substance I find less and less these days. Have a sincere thank you and a thumbs up!

author: @flotsamike
text: The US drinking water standard for nickel 63 is 8 Bq per liter.  The 100 micro Watt nickel 63 power source has at least 1800  Bq in it.   All things being equal than a 1 watt power source would have 18 million Bq.  If improperly disposed of, and you assume only 1% of the activity contaminates water it would contaminate 1.8 million liters  above the drinking water standard. That's for 1 watt   initial output. Luckily there's not enough nickel 63 in the world to make too many kilowatts level power sources. It would be a good use for the 9  graphite block re actors at Hanford  that are  heavily contaminated with both Nickel 63 and carbon 14.  And the 22 graphite block reactors in the UK.

author: @zackbranco9691
text: There appears to be a bit of misunderstanding regarding the half-life of a radioactive material. Half-life simply refers to half of the radioactive material breaking down into something else. It seems 10 half-lives is the aggreed upon number of half-lives before the amount of radioactive material remaining is negligible, assuming it didn't break down into another radioactive material. Of course, the output of the battery will continuously drop over those 10 half-lives, but the americicum-241 battery would still have power in 432 years and 864 years, assuming the rest of the components held up.

author: @splaytrees
text:
Seventy microamps (70 µA) is a very small amount of current, but it's not insignificant in the context of low-power electronics and microcontrollers. Here's what you could potentially power with 70 µA:

Low-Power Microcontrollers: Many modern microcontrollers, especially those designed for battery-operated devices, have very low power sleep modes. For example, some models can operate in a sleep or standby mode on less than 70 µA, waking up at intervals to perform tasks.

Watches or Small LCD Displays: Traditional digital watches or small liquid crystal display (LCD) screens can operate on very low current, often in the range of microamps, making them potential candidates for operation at this current level.

CMOS Integrated Circuits: CMOS technology, which is used in a wide range of electronic components, is known for its low power consumption. Certain CMOS devices or circuits, especially when operating in low-power or standby modes, could potentially be powered by 70 µA.

Sensors: Some types of low-power sensors, such as temperature or light sensors used in various applications including weather stations, smart homes, or IoT devices, might operate under 70 µA depending on their design and the operational mode.

LEDs: While most LEDs require more current for standard brightness, high-efficiency LEDs or micro LEDs can sometimes be operated at very low currents for applications like indicator lights. However, at 70 µA, the light would be very dim, perhaps only visible in total darkness.

Quartz Crystal Oscillators: These components, which provide the clock signal for devices like microcontrollers and watches, can operate on very low power. Some quartz oscillators might work within this current range, especially in devices optimized for low power consumption.

author: @project-326
text:
its very entertaining but you are just seeing light leakage.  If you perform the experiment in a slightly more scientific way (ie really remove ambient light) you will not see any difference if the Am-241 source is present of not.
The semiconductor die is passivated with  layer of silicon dioxide (quartz glass) and the alpha particles will not be able to pass that and reach the semiconductor layers below.   Even if they could pass through the passivization layer, they certainly could not pass though the first layer of N-type material to reach the N-P junction where the charge carriers are produced.  Light is a photon and behaves differently in this semiconductor and can generate hole-electron pairs on the surface layers.  Betavolt uses BETA radiation, not alpha as you are getting from the Am-241 source.  In a BetaVoltiac or AlphaVoltiac device, the source of the electrons (beta) or holes (alpha) needs to be one of the contacts, the semiconductor is the other contact.

replies.author: @AlterKater
replies.text: Alphas easily pass 1 micron of aluminium, the higer energy alphas up to 5 micron of aluminium, and the passivation layer  is thin enough. Lots of semiconductor Alpha sensors and Radon monitors on the market, you can buy them off the shelf.

replies.author: @project-326
replies.text:
There will be a roughly 1-2 MeV energy loss from crossing 1 micron aluminum, which represents about 20-40% of the theoretical starting energy of an Am-241 decay alpha.  In practice, the gold coating on the source, recoil losses and the few millimeters of air will also reduce the alpha energy even further.
What needs to be remembered that a three layer device like an NPN transistor has the (usually) emitter on the top of the of the die, so to reach the junction, the alphas would have to first pass the SiO2 passivization layer and then get through the emitter n-doped layer.  That is a substantial mount of material.
Alpha detectors that are based on semiconductors as usually 2 layer devices, often PIN diodes.  Not NPN power transistors...

author: @RWBHere
text: Thanks Robert. There is also some low energy Gamma radiation from the decay of Americium-241, so it's worth remembering. The smoke detector works by generating a voltage between two plates at each side of an air cavity. Smoke particles enter the cavity and disrupt (absorb) the Alpha particles, causing a drop in voltage, and triggering the alarm. So the detector is also essentially a low power battery.

text: Is the Betavolt cell "100 milliWatts", or "100 microWatts"?  You said "milli-", but the photo at 2:13 showed the Greek "mu" prefix, meaning "micro".  That photo is NOT the new Betavolt cell, but it's hard to believe that Betavolt has increased the power output by a full 1000x, and other on-line sources say "microWatts".  So, when they claim that they'll scale it up to 1W, that's a factor of 10,000, not just a factor of 10.  Also, the BV100 MSRP is $500, so scaling it up 10,000x makes it a $5,000,000 cell phone battery.

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
Very interesting. It didn't occur to me until now that alpha radiations, being near the UV wavelengths,  could cause photovoltaic cells or semiconductors to generate a voltage. I guess the same is true of x-rays too since they're the next step up from UV. Not that I know of any material which puts out x-rays while decaying.
With the Americium, it looks like quite a few pieces would be needed in series to get 3.3V. I suppose each pellet could produce twice the output if it's extracted from its casing, to expose the underside and to sandwich ir between two transistors.
My smoke detector with Americium is definitely putting out Beta radiations too as I get an increase of over 500 CPMs when pointing my Geiger counter towards the plastic case and Alpha radiation wouldn't make it through the plastic and thin metal cage surrounding the ionization chamber. I don't think it puts out Gamma though because the readings drop when I insert a 1mm steel plate between the smoke detector and the Geiger counter. It's still bad though if it can ionize floating dust which might end up being inhaled.

author: @greenaum
text:
There's a difference between the slow, what are called "thermal neutrons" in a reactor, and them being hot. They're actually less energetic than the fast ones, which as you mention kinda bugger off, though sometimes a reactor tries to slow neutrons down with a moderator material. This is because slower neutrons are better at smashing open other atoms, to give the chain reaction a reactor relies on. Where fast ones tend not to interact as much.

It's actually un-needed Strong Force, binding energy, that is the "mass" lost as energy in nuclear fission. The number of particles left after is the same as before, they're just arranged into smaller atoms, an overall less-energetic state. The excess energy is release as heat, but mass is lost because binding energy actually contributes quite a bit to the weight of an atom, even though it isn't a solid particle. Because energy = mass (times the speed of light squared). That equation works both ways.

author: @greenaum
text:
Round about 0:30 you mention the battery giving 100 milliwatts of power, then later around 2:30 it's 100 MICROwatts! It's rather important to get this right! 3 orders of magnitude is a big deal in electronics! From what I've heard elsewhere the correct figure seems to be 100 microwatts. Useful for extremely low-power radio with sensors and the like, in super power-saving deep sleep modes, and maybe charging up a capacitor for occasional bursts of more power. 

You mention a future 1 watt version, so that'd be 10,000x more than these current ones. Although they're only tiny, scaling up by ten in each direction to a 15cm cube would be 3,000x bigger. So then you'd need to decide which dimension to increase by 3x, or whatever you like, to scale it up. That's still a very reasonable size for a battery with real power. 

This nickel isotope sounds very energetic, I wonder if that's going to cause problems in itself? I'm presuming, of all things, they're not gonna use LEAD in the shielding after removing every atom of it from electronics otherwise. Covet your precious lead solder, the stuff that actually joins components together!

author: @paulbeckett6942
text:
Downside of using silicon junction commercially is lattice damage. It will work but quickly degrade, within days or weeks as more atoms get moved out of position in the junction.  Diamond is much better as the lattice is inherently more resistant to damage. That’s why it is used here. It will last long enough to be commercially viable.
Two more items: I’m pretty sure Americium is an alpha source primarily (around 85%).  And, by the way, don’t do this experiment unless you know what you are doing. Especially don’t cut/crush the pellet. Breathing Americium dust is **very** bad.  In contrast Ni63 emits beta particles as described in the excellent presentation here. At their lower energies, the “penetration” depth is measured in micron. They don’t even make it out of the package, so the battery is quite safe. Even were you to break it open (good luck with that), beta particles would struggle to make it past the surface layer of your skin. So, again, quite safe. Contrast with toxic lithium.

At 100 micro-amps at 3 volts, so it's 0.3 milli-watts. Over 50 years, that's 95 Wh. I read that the price of a NDB is based on USD 2200 per watt. So, at 0.3 milli-watts, they would be USD 0.66 each. That's a cost of about USD 6.9 per kWh. Seems likely that it can be manufactured for much lower per-unit street price if competition were allowed.

But since this comes from China and China doesn't actually enforce other country's patents (or any intellectual property rights for that matter) there is no "good" reason for other countries to enforce China's patents. So, from the rest of the world's point of view their technology should be treated as public domain so that any company can manufacture them, which should keep local price low by competition.

replies.author: @alden1132
replies.text: The reason they produce such a low voltage is because the amount of power they produce is limited by the rate of decay of the radioactive source. No matter how much of the the energy the decay puts out, the rate of decay doesn't change. There's a finite amount of energy that can eventually be extracted, but no way to increase the rate at which the matter decays. Well, unless you're talking about fissile material reaching criticality, but then you're not dealing with a battery, but a reactor...or a bomb.

author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: So if my math is correct, assuming 1mA at 110 mV, it should produce 9.504 Joules of energy over 24 hours. For a red LED operating at a F.V. of 2V with a current draw of 20 mA, the energy if stored with no losses or leakage would be enough to keep it lit for 237.6 seconds or ~4 minutes. This could prove to be extremely useful for supplying enough power to actuate some relays (or beefy transistors) and wake up hibernating electronics in applications where they’ll remain turned off for extended periods of time. 4 of these nuclear batteries could maintain a 1000mAh NiMH battery at 80% state of charge for 156 years. Or maybe you could create an atomic clock utilising the time-averaged regularity of the decays.

replies.author: @EvenTheDogAgrees
replies.text:
No, it can't. What you can do is use it to charge a conventional battery during "off-time", for devices that require power only intermittently. And yes, I said "battery" and not "capacitor", because capacitors can only store relatively small amounts of energy.

However, a far more efficient way to achieve this is by using a bog standard charger if you live in a first world country and have access to mains voltage Or a dynamo built into the device, like with some torches or radios, if access to mains voltage is problematic (no infrastructure, or during a power outage). Or use solar panels if it's a device in a remote location where you don't have mains and winding the dynamo is impractical. E.g. a LoRa node monitoring some environmental parameters in a remote location in a forest, or what have you.

I get it, these batteries are cool. But they're not a solution to every problem that has "power" in the title. There's a place for these things for sure, but powering devices that require more power than they can deliver is not it.
